raw:
{
  "weights": {
    "R1.1": 0.3,
    "R1.2": 0.7,
    "R1.3": 1.0,
    "R1.4": 0.7,
    "R1.5": 0.3,
    "R1.6": 0.7,
    "R1.7": 0.0,
    "RX.1": 1.0,
    "RX.2": 1.0,
    "RX.3": 1.0,
    "RX.4": 1.0,
    "RX.5": 1.0
  },
  "active_set": ["R1.2", "R1.3", "R1.4", "R1.6", "RX.1", "RX.2", "RX.3", "RX.4", "RX.5"],
  "category_focus": "R1",
  "clarification_needed": true,
  "clarification_question": "Should the check focus on (A) writing polish/clarity only, (B) technical substance—logical consistency, evidence-claim alignment, and novelty assertions—without copyediting, or (C) both A and B?",
  "rationale": {
    "R1.2": "A general 'check' typically includes verifying that the motivation, method, and conclusions form a coherent argument chain.",
    "R1.3": "Because the abstract reports concrete quantitative outcomes, checking that the stated claims align with the presented evidence is load-bearing.",
    "R1.4": "The text makes 'first' claims, so assessing likely novelty relative to nearest prior work is a central expectation of a substantive check.",
    "R1.6": "Requests to 'check' short passages often include sentence-level clarity and grammar, making writing polish a central responsibility.",
    "RX.1": "We should disclose uncertainty arising from the brevity of the artifact (an abstract) and lack of citations or full paper context.",
    "RX.2": "We must avoid overclaiming beyond what the provided text supports, especially regarding performance and 'first' assertions.",
    "RX.3": "We will adhere strictly to defining responsibilities and not perform edits, experiments, or new analyses.",
    "RX.4": "We will avoid guidance that could encourage unsafe deployment or misrepresentation of safety justifications in control settings.",
    "RX.5": "All judgments will be explicitly tied to the provided delegation and the verbatim abstract to maintain traceability."
  }
}

parsed.clarification_question: Should the check focus on (A) writing polish/clarity only, (B) technical substance—logical consistency, evidence-claim alignment, and novelty assertions—without copyediting, or (C) both A and B?

rationale.R1.2: A general 'check' typically includes verifying that the motivation, method, and conclusions form a coherent argument chain.
rationale.R1.3: Because the abstract reports concrete quantitative outcomes, checking that the stated claims align with the presented evidence is load-bearing.
rationale.R1.4: The text makes 'first' claims, so assessing likely novelty relative to nearest prior work is a central expectation of a substantive check.
rationale.R1.6: Requests to 'check' short passages often include sentence-level clarity and grammar, making writing polish a central responsibility.
rationale.RX.1: We should disclose uncertainty arising from the brevity of the artifact (an abstract) and lack of citations or full paper context.
rationale.RX.2: We must avoid overclaiming beyond what the provided text supports, especially regarding performance and 'first' assertions.
rationale.RX.3: We will adhere strictly to defining responsibilities and not perform edits, experiments, or new analyses.
rationale.RX.4: We will avoid guidance that could encourage unsafe deployment or misrepresentation of safety justifications in control settings.
rationale.RX.5: All judgments will be explicitly tied to the provided delegation and the verbatim abstract to maintain traceability.
